data.content:
Here are the benchmark results comparing **.NET 9** vs **.NET 10**:

| Method | .NET 9 | .NET 10 | Δ | Alloc |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AverageWordLength_Small** | 129.7 ns | 24.7 ns | **~5× faster** | 0 B |
| **AverageWordLength_Large** | 5,106.9 ns | 2,638.3 ns | **~2× faster** | 0 B |
| **WordLengthHistogram_Small** | 162.7 ns | 135.7 ns | ~1.2× faster | 72 B |
| **WordLengthHistogram_Large** | 9,616.1 ns | 12,898.4 ns | ~0.7× (slower) | 96 B |
| **UniqueWordCount_Small** | 370.3 ns | 1,232.9 ns | ~0.3× (slower) | 760 B |
| **UniqueWordCount_Large** | 28,002.5 ns | 205,840.6 ns | ~0.14× (slower) | 73,152 B |

**⚠️ Important caveat:** This machine is under heavy load — the error margins are extremely wide (often exceeding 100% of the mean), especially for .NET 10 runs. The apparent regressions in `UniqueWordCount` and `WordLengthHistogram_Large` are likely **noise from system contention**, not real regressions. Memory allocations are identical across both runtimes, which is a good sign.

**Recommendation:** Re-run these benchmarks on a quiet, dedicated machine to get reliable numbers before drawing conclusions about the .NET 9→10 upgrade impact.
